source_inspiration: Felix 2018 Q5 — disc tower top view
stem_template: {name} looks at a tower of {N} stacked discs from directly above. How many discs can {pronoun_subject_lower} see?

visual_manifest.art_brief: A tower of {N} discs stacked from bottom to top. The bottom disc is the widest, each disc above is slightly smaller. Show the 3D tower from a slight angle so all discs are visible, with an arrow indicating 'view from above'.
visual_manifest.alt_text: A 3D stack of {N} discs of decreasing size.

misconceptions.feedback_child: The top disc is smallest! The bigger ones underneath stick out. Can you see their edges?
Almost all of them! Even the very bottom one is wider — its edge peeks out.
You can always see at least the top! And the bigger ones below peek out too.

socratic_feedback.on_correct: Great eye for perspective! Because the bottom discs are wider, their edges are still visible from the top.
socratic_feedback.incorrect_one: Think about it — the top disc is the smallest. The ones underneath are wider. Can their edges be seen from above?
socratic_feedback.generic_incorrect: Imagine stacking plates of different sizes. If you look down from above, can you see the edges of the bigger plates?
socratic_feedback.encouragement_after_retry: Try stacking some coins of different sizes and looking straight down. What do you see?

author: AP
